quote: The limits of my language mean the limits of my world.
through_line: Language shapes what we see — and what we think AI is. When we call a model a ‘digital brain’ that ‘thinks’ and ‘feels,’ we’re not just simplifying; we’re choosing who gets held accountable. Today’s brief sits with the limits of our metaphors, and with the practice of ethical sensitivity as one antidote.

articles.title: What will AI mean for economic inequality?
articles.pitch: The economic gains from AI are accumulating fastest for workers and firms already advantaged. MIT Technology Review examines the structural dynamics driving this — why AI acts as a force multiplier that risks widening, not closing, existing income and opportunity gaps.

articles.title: Five Practical Questions Every DMO Should Ask When Building an AI Policy
articles.pitch: Before deploying any AI tool, every DMO needs clarity on five foundational questions — about governance, data ownership, transparency, and staff capacity. Destinations International turns policy-building from an abstract obligation into a practical checklist.

articles.title: Destination Marketing Trends for 2026: Travel Trends and Shifts Shaping DMOs
articles.pitch: Six trends shaping how DMOs need to reposition heading into 2026: Answer Engine Optimization, data-driven storytelling, emotional differentiation, fragmented discovery journeys. Watauga’s annual read is grounded, practitioner-focused, and worth the time.

articles.title: ‘Digital brains’ that ‘think’ and ‘feel’: why do we personify AI models, and are these metaphors actually helpful?
articles.pitch: Every time we say a model ‘understands’ or ‘feels’ or ‘cares,’ we’re loading the metaphor with assumptions about accountability. This piece maps the conceptual risks of AI anthropomorphism — and offers a more precise vocabulary for talking about what systems actually do.

articles.title: Ethical Sensitivity
articles.pitch: Ethical sensitivity — the ability to feel the moral weight of a situation before deliberating about it — is something that can be trained. Fronsdal argues it’s the most important foundation of ethical life, and that practice, not rules, is how it deepens.

articles.title: What your mind’s blank moments reveal about consciousness
articles.pitch: Moments when your mind goes truly blank — with no content whatsoever — challenge one of the oldest assumptions about consciousness: that to be conscious is always to be conscious of something. Neuroscientist Thomas Andrillon explores what these silences reveal about the nature of mind.
